categoryDescription: Evaluates the ETF's past performance — multi-year returns, recent momentum, calendar-year consistency, AUM growth trend, and standing within its Morningstar category peer group. Five generic factors are applied to every ETF group; group-specific perspective (right benchmark, mandate-alignment test, technical-trend reading where decision-useful, distribution / NAV-erosion checks where applicable, futures-roll-cost call-out, daily-leverage decay arithmetic) lives in each factor's `groupInstructions`. Each factor's `factorAnalysisDescription` carries the generic measurement principle and the mandate-based Pass/Fail rule; `groupInstructions` is the authoritative perspective rule for the fund's group — when the two appear to conflict, group instructions win.

factors.factorDescription: Measures multi-year compound growth (5 / 10 / 15 / 20Y CAGR and trailing returns). The mandate-alignment test lives here too — comparing CAGR to the group-appropriate benchmark over multi-year windows is how we judge whether a style premium, thematic tilt, leveraged target, or strategy promise actually delivered. For young funds (under 5Y) use only the periods available and flag the limited history. Pass when CAGR is at or above the group benchmark across most windows, or within tracking tolerance for a passive index fund. Fail when CAGR materially trails across multiple long-window comparisons without a mandate-based explanation.
factors.factorMetrics: cagr5y, cagr10y, cagr15y, cagr20y, return5y, return10y, returnsAnnual, returnsTrailing, indexName

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare CAGR to the broad index the fund tracks (S&P 500 for US large blend, MSCI EAFE for foreign developed, MSCI ACWI for global, Russell 1000 Growth / Value for US style tilts). For style-tilted funds (value, growth, dividend, low-vol, quality), this IS the factor-premium delivery test — the tilt should outperform the parent index over multi-year windows when its style is in favor. Plain Large Blend / Total Market should sit within tracking tolerance of S&P 500.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare CAGR to both the sector / theme index AND the S&P 500 — the S&P 500 cross-check is the mandate test (a 'theme' that just tracked the broad market over 10Y has not delivered the thesis it sold). For single-country EM, use the country index (MSCI China, Nifty 50, MSCI Brazil) plus MSCI Emerging Markets as the diversified-EM reference.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Long-horizon CAGR is the daily-reset decay test — quote the underlying's CAGR × stated leverage factor as the textbook expectation, then show the realized result and frame the gap as compounding decay. State explicitly: these are short-term trading vehicles, never buy-and-hold; the 'how much would $10k be today' question is the wrong frame. Do not compare to the S&P 500 directly here.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare CAGR to the spot benchmark (LBMA Gold, COMEX silver, Bloomberg Commodity Index, CME CF BTC / ETH Reference Rate) AND the S&P 500. For futures-based commodity wrappers, the spot-vs-fund gap is structural NAV erosion from contango / roll cost — call it out by name (USO lost ~94% of NAV from inception due to roll cost). For physical-backed gold / silver and spot crypto, note tracking efficiency vs spot (custody / management drag).
groupInstructions.derivative-income: For these strategy-driven funds, long-term CAGR is the mandate test: covered-call funds (JEPI, QYLD, SPYI) should deliver yield + capped upside + cushion in down markets (verify all three); long-short / market-neutral should show equity-like-or-lower CAGR with materially lower drawdown; managed-futures should show crisis alpha in 2008 / 2020 / 2022 specifically. Compare TOTAL return (including distributions) to both the underlying equity index and a high-dividend equity ETF. Flag NAV erosion: a flat or negative 10Y price return with positive total return = distributions partly funded by return-of-capital.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare CAGR to a same-period 60/40 (S&P 500 + Bloomberg US Aggregate) and to the allocation peer-category median. Mandate-band check: moderate ~5-7%, conservative ~4-5%, aggressive ~7-9%; target-date funds should track the issuer's published glide path. For tactical / global allocation, the question is whether active calls beat a passive 60/40 over multi-year windows.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare CAGR to the Bloomberg US Aggregate (broad core) or a duration-matched Treasury index (ICE BofA 1-3Y / 7-10Y / 20+Y Treasury). For muni funds, compare to the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index and quote tax-equivalent CAGR at a stated bracket (~32% federal baseline plus state exemption for in-state holders). For TIPS, use a TIPS index and separate real vs nominal returns.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare CAGR to the appropriate credit benchmark — Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield, S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan, ICE BofA US Preferred Stock, JPM EMBI Global Diversified, ICE BofA US Convertible. Quote a same-period 60/40 CAGR too — retail's honest question is whether they were actually paid for taking real default and subordination risk.

factors.factorDescription: Evaluates recent performance over 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y. Distinguishes short-term momentum (1M, 3M) from trailing performance (6M, 1Y) — a strong 1Y alongside weak recent months is usually a normal pullback within an uptrend, not broad weakness. For the three timing-relevant groups (sector / thematic, leveraged / inverse, commodities / digital), this factor ALSO carries the technical-trend reading (MA20 / 50 / 150 / 200, RSI daily / weekly / monthly, distance from 52w high / low and ATH / ATL) because entry timing genuinely matters there; for the other five groups MA / RSI signals are noise and the technical commentary should stay brief. Every short-term number needs the same-period number for the group-appropriate benchmark. Pass when short-term performance is in line with or ahead of the group benchmark and the trend / breakout direction is decision-useful for the fund's typical holding horizon. Fail when materially lagging the group benchmark across multiple recent windows without a mandate reason.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return6m, returnYtd, return1y, returnsAnnual, ma20, ma50, ma150, ma200, ma50ChgPercent, ma200ChgPercent, rsi, rsiW, rsiM, high52wChg, low52wChg, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the tracked index (S&P 500, MSCI EAFE, MSCI ACWI, Russell 1000 style index). Flag whether near-term weakness is fund-specific (lagging the index) or a broad-market move that hit every peer. MA / RSI signals are usually noise for buy-and-hold broad-equity — keep technical commentary brief unless price is at a clear extreme (monthly RSI >70 or <30).
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare to both the sector / theme index AND the S&P 500 over each window — retail wants to see whether the sector bet is currently outperforming the broad market or fading. Sector cycles drive forward returns — read MA50 / MA200 position and RSI to gauge whether the current entry is timing-favorable; flag overbought (monthly RSI >70) or oversold (<30); tie to the sector's macro cycle (rate-sensitive sectors near a rate pivot, energy near supply news, etc.).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Short-term return IS the entire decision frame. Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the stated daily-leverage multiple of the unleveraged underlying — a 3x fund should be roughly 3x the underlying's same-period move minus reset slippage; flag any large gap as path-dependency loss. Suitability is short-term directional trading — read price vs MA20 / 50 / 150 / 200 and RSI levels (monthly RSI >75 = stretched even by trading standards); frame current entry vs 52-week range.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare to the spot underlying return (LBMA Gold, BCOM, CME CF BTC / ETH Reference Rate) over each window. For futures-based commodity wrappers, show the spot vs fund gap and call out roll cost (contango / backwardation) explicitly. Momentum-driven asset classes — quote MA50 / MA200 position and RSI levels (monthly RSI >70 stretched, <30 washout); frame current position vs ATH / 52-week range.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y TOTAL return to the underlying equity index the strategy writes options on (S&P 500 for JEPI / SPYI, Nasdaq-100 for QYLD / XYLD / QQQI). Distribution composition matters here — option-premium income, ordinary dividends, short-term capital gains, return of capital. Headline yield alone misleads; flag whether ROC is propping the yield while NAV erodes. MA / RSI is noise — keep technical commentary minimal.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to a same-period 60/40 blend (S&P 500 + Bloomberg US Aggregate) and to the allocation peer-category median. For target-date funds, also compare to the issuer's published peer group for the dated year. MA / RSI signals are noise for allocation funds — keep technical commentary minimal.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to a duration-matched Treasury or core-bond index. For muni, compare to a national muni index of similar duration. Flag whether near-term moves are rate-driven (parallel with peers) or fund-specific (active calls, tracking drift). Distribution should track SEC yield — large divergence signals smoothing. MA / RSI is noise here.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the matching credit benchmark (Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield, S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan, ICE BofA US Preferred Stock, JPM EMBI Global Diversified, ICE BofA US Convertible). Flag whether short-term weakness is spread-widening that hit the whole sub-asset class or fund-specific. Distribution moves with reference rates (SOFR-linked coupons reset quarterly) and default cycles.

factors.factorDescription: Assesses how stable returns have been across periods and calendar years. Quote calendar-year hit rate (how often the fund had a positive year), worst single year, and percentile-rank trend year-over-year (cite the actual movement, e.g. `14 → 87 → 18`, not just 'volatile'). The benchmark-matched bad year rule applies: a passive fund whose worst year is in line with its index and category is not a Fail — that is the asset class moving, not fund failure. For income-paying funds, this factor also captures distribution stability over time — whether the headline yield held up, ratcheted down, or was propped up by return-of-capital. A 'consistent' total return that sits on top of a steadily eroding NAV is not real consistency. Pass when the calendar-year pattern fits the group's typical dispersion and distributions (where relevant) held up. Fail when the fund swings materially harder than the group benchmark, OR distributions were cut sharply, OR total return is being propped up by ROC.
factors.factorMetrics: returnsAnnual, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, percentileRanks, dividendTtm, divGrowth3y, divGrowth5y, divYears, divGrYears, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, change1y, change5y, change10y

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Show calendar-year hit rate, worst single year, and percentile-rank trend against the S&P 500 / tracked index. A 5-year migration up or down the percentile ladder matters more than any single year. For broad-equity income / dividend tilts (High Dividend Yield, Dividend Aristocrats-style), also check whether the distribution held up year-over-year.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector and country returns swing harder than the broad market — quote the worst single year (sectors regularly drop 30-50% in their bad year; single-country EM can drop 40-50%) and whether that lined up with broad-market bad years or was sector-specific. Quote the same-period S&P 500 pattern for contrast.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Consistency is structurally poor — quote calendar-year wins vs losses, the worst single year (-50% to -90% common in adverse years), and recovery duration. Retail needs to see in plain numbers that consistency is not a design feature of these products; reinforce the short-term-only warning every iteration.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Extreme calendar-year dispersion — gold a few +25-30% years amid multi-year flat stretches; broad commodities ±20-30% swings; BTC routinely ±50-100% in a single year. Compare hit rate vs S&P 500 over the same window so retail sees the trade-off. For futures-based wrappers, a steadily declining 5Y / 10Y price with positive distribution-yielded total return = roll-cost NAV erosion in disguise.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Show how yield + capped upside translated into total return across each calendar year, including down years where option premium did NOT offset the underlying loss. Quote per-share distribution year-by-year, the latest-year ROC share, and divergence between total return and price-only return — a flat-to-positive total return on top of a steadily declining NAV is structural NAV erosion (QYLD is the textbook case). Worst calendar year vs both the equity benchmark AND a high-dividend equity ETF.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Smooth-ride delivery is the core mandate — quote calendar-year hit rate, the worst single year, and the same-period 100% S&P 500 worst year side-by-side (a moderate-allocation fund should have a materially smaller worst year than pure equity). For target-date glide paths, compare to the issuer's own glide-path target. The bond-sleeve distribution should track its yield — flag any abrupt drops.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Quote calendar-year hit rate and the worst calendar year (2022 was the rate-shock test — long IG / government / muni lost -25% to -31%, intermediate core ~-13%, ultrashort / MMF ~+1%). Compare worst year to a duration-matched Treasury index. Distribution should closely track SEC yield; large gaps signal smoothing or de-accumulated coupons.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Quote calendar-year hit rate and worst single year (2020 COVID: HY -15-20%, bank loans -5-10%, EM debt -15%, preferreds -15-25%; 2022 hit longer-duration credit harder). Per-share distribution year-by-year, especially around 2020 / 2022; break out ROC share for preferreds and convertibles where structurally larger. Compare worst year to the matching credit benchmark.

factors.factorDescription: Tracks the trajectory of the fund's AUM (assets under management) over time — is it growing, flat, or shrinking? AUM trend is a market vote on the fund: rising AUM signals retail and institutional adoption; sustained outflows signal lost confidence in the strategy or asset class. Below ~$50M AUM, fund closure / merger risk becomes real (issuer may liquidate, merge into a sibling fund, or stop marketing). Conversely, very large AUM in narrow / illiquid strategies (single-country EM, small-cap niche, capacity-constrained thematics) can hit capacity limits and hurt forward performance — a known concern for funds that grew past their natural strategy capacity. Use current AUM from the data; for trend, look up historical AUM (1Y / 3Y / 5Y / since inception) from Morningstar / etf.com / the issuer fact sheet via the Factor-metric lookup rule. Strong recent return often correlates with strong inflows (and vice versa), so AUM trend doubles as a market-validated read on the last year's performance. Pass when AUM is at or above category-typical levels AND trend is flat-to-rising. Fail when AUM is well below the survival threshold for the category, OR has been on sustained sharp decline (50%+ from peak) not explainable by an asset-class decline of similar magnitude.
factors.factorMetrics: aum, sharesOut, volume, avgVolume, dollarVol, inceptionDate, return1y

groupInstructions.broad-equity: For mega broad-market funds (SPY ~$500B+, VTI, IVV), AUM trend matters mainly for fee competitiveness and liquidity depth — small relative moves are not decision-changing. For newer or niche broad-equity funds (factor tilts, themed dividend ETFs, foreign / single-region funds), sustained outflows signal lost confidence in the tilt. Below $100M for newer funds = closure risk worth flagging.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: AUM in thematic ETFs is highly cyclical — money piles in at theme peaks (clean energy 2020-21, robotics 2018-21, AI 2023-24) and out at troughs; outflows of 60-80% from peak are common after a theme falls out of favor. What matters more for retail is whether the fund is at closure risk (below ~$50M) vs just out-of-favor. Single-country EM funds also need enough AUM for trading liquidity (bid-ask widens fast on thin AUM).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Leveraged products see fast AUM swings tied to retail sentiment and short-term performance — TQQQ flows mirror Nasdaq sentiment, SQQQ picks up in bear markets. Below $50M, closure / merger risk is real (multiple leveraged products have been delisted over the years). High AUM in a small-cap or narrow-sector leveraged product can hurt daily-tracking quality due to derivative-position limits.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Commodity / crypto wrappers see AUM swings with the underlying — GLD AUM tracks gold sentiment; spot-BTC ETFs (IBIT, FBTC) tracked BTC adoption post-spot-ETF approval. For futures-based commodity funds, sustained AUM signals roll cost has been absorbable; shrinking AUM in a roll-cost-burdened fund is a closure warning. For single-commodity wrappers (oil, natural gas), thin AUM amplifies tracking issues.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: AUM growth has been a defining story for derivative-income funds in the 2020s — JEPI grew from $20M to $30B+, QYLD has run at $5-10B, SPYI at $5B+. Sustained AUM growth signals retail income demand. Sudden outflows can signal the strategy stopped delivering or NAV erosion finally caught retail attention — read AUM trend alongside the NAV / distribution trajectory.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Target-date funds have AUM driven mostly by 401(k) defaults — most flows are mechanical and sticky. Look for unusual outflows that may signal advisor / plan-sponsor migration to a different family (common for actively-managed target-date series losing market share to cheaper passive). For tactical / global allocation, AUM trend is retail vote on the strategy.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Bond-fund AUM moves with rate-cycle sentiment — IG core / Treasury flows surged 2023-2024 as rates peaked; muni flows are tied to tax-bracket investor demand. For ultrashort / MMF, AUM is sticky cash-substitute money that grows in late-cycle and bleeds when rates fall. Below $100M, even bond funds face closure risk.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-fund AUM is more volatile — HY / bank-loan / EM-debt flows surge in risk-on periods and bleed in risk-off; preferred / convertible flows mirror equity sentiment. AUM trend tells you whether retail / institutional money is currently rewarded for taking the credit risk. Below $50M = closure risk.

factors.factorDescription: Where does this fund sit within its Morningstar category peer group across 1Y / 3Y / 5Y / 10Y? Use percentile and quartile rank — top quartile is above-average, second quartile near-average-but-better, third quartile below-average-but-acceptable, bottom quartile materially weak. Always report peer-group size alongside the rank — median in a 600-fund category is very different from median in a 10-fund category. Track whether the fund's standing is improving, stable, or deteriorating; cite the actual percentile movement. For passive index funds inside an active-heavy peer category, structural tracking-cost headwind means median-among-active is a Pass-grade outcome for a low-cost index fund — do not Fail a passive fund on peer rank alone. Pass when the fund is in the top two quartiles over the longest available window AND the percentile trend is not deteriorating sharply. Fail when it sits in the bottom quartile across multiple windows without a mandate-based explanation (e.g., a long-vol fund in the bottom quartile of a multi-strategy category during an equity bull is mandate-aligned, not a Fail).
factors.factorMetrics: percentileRanks, quartileRanks, numberOfInvestmentsInCategory, overviewCategory, returnVsCategory, riskVsCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Large Blend, Large Value, Large Growth, Mid / Small Blend, Foreign Large Blend, Global Large-Stock Blend etc. are separate Morningstar categories — compare to peers in the SAME category. For passive index funds in an active-heavy peer category (most active manager peer groups), median is a Pass-grade outcome because active managers carry a structural fee + tracking-cost headwind.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector categories are tight (Technology, Health, Real Estate, Utilities, Communications); thematic and miscellaneous-sector categories are more dispersed (Miscellaneous Sector, Theme, Equity Digital Assets). Single-country EM categories have small peer counts (India Equity, China Region, Latin America Stock) — report the count alongside the rank.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Trading-Leveraged Equity, Trading-Inverse Equity, Trading-Leveraged Debt, etc. are small peer categories. Rank between products inside a leverage bucket is mostly about daily-tracking quality and issuer execution — structural decay applies to every product in the category, so do not Fail a fund on rank alone if the decay is in line with peers.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Categories are small (Commodities Broad Basket, Commodities Precious Metals, Digital Assets, Single Currency) — report peer count. Category dispersion is wide; rank reads with that caveat. Physical-backed vs futures-based wrappers within the same category have different cost / tracking profiles — note which sub-group of peers the rank actually compares against.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Derivative Income, Defined Outcome, Equity Hedged, Multistrategy, Systematic Trend, Long-Short Equity, Market Neutral, Macro Trading, Multialternative, Event Driven, Relative Value Arbitrage, Volatility, Downside Hedge are separate Morningstar categories — compare to the right one. Within Derivative Income especially, peer dispersion is wide (JEPI vs QYLD vs SPYI have different option mechanics and underlying indices).
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Each target-date vintage is its own peer group (Target-Date 2030, 2040, 2050, 2055, 2060, 2065+, Target-Date Retirement); allocation buckets are split too (Conservative / Moderate / Aggressive / Moderately Conservative / Moderately Aggressive Allocation, Tactical Allocation, Global Allocation variants, Target Risk, Target Outcome). Stay inside the specific category.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Bond categories are duration-defined (Ultrashort Bond, Short-Term Bond, Intermediate Core Bond, Intermediate Core-Plus, Long-Term Bond, Short / Intermediate / Long Government, Inflation-Protected Bond, Short-Term Inflation-Protected). Muni categories split by national vs single-state and by maturity (Muni National Short / Interm / Long, Muni California / NY / NJ / MA / OH / MN). Compare within the exact category.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: High Yield Bond, Bank Loan, Preferred Stock, Convertibles, Emerging Markets Bond, Emerging-Markets Local-Currency Bond, Multisector Bond, Nontraditional Bond, High Yield Muni, Securitized Bond-Focused, Broad Credit, Private Debt-General — each is its own category. Compare within the specific one.
